summary: Device policies for Clipboard History and cross-device clipboard sync. The local Microsoft OSPolicy.admx file explicitly documents the same policy key, value names, and enabled/disabled values that the app uses for this bundle.

setting.name: Clipboard History and Cross-Device Clipboard Sync
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can keep a history of copied items and can sync clipboard data across devices. These two policy values decide whether those features are allowed.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable these policies for privacy, compliance, or to avoid cloud-backed clipboard sync. Some teams still want local clipboard history while blocking cross-device sync.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning both policies off removes Clipboard History and cross-device clipboard sync. Leaving them not configured lets Windows and the user settings decide the final behavior.
setting.professional_notes: These are machine policy values under HKLM and are appropriate for device-level management. The local OSPolicy.admx file defines both values under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. They describe whether Windows is allowed to expose the features, not every possible Settings app state.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows policy does not force the feature. The user-facing behavior stays under normal Windows control.

allowed_values.label: Disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Clipboard History is blocked by policy.

allowed_values.label: Enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Clipboard History is explicitly allowed by policy.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows policy does not force clipboard sync. The final behavior stays under normal Windows control.

allowed_values.label: Disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Cross-device clipboard sync is blocked by policy.

allowed_values.label: Enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Cross-device clipboard sync is explicitly allowed by policy.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current implementation writes both policy values to 0, which matches the documented disable behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows policy default

states.rationale: Default policy state is not configured.
Default policy state is not configured.

windows_defaults.notes: This describes policy state, not the end-user Settings app toggle default.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: People who want Windows to decide feature availability normally
avoid_for: Managed devices that require clipboard restrictions

states.rationale: Keeps the device at the normal Windows policy baseline.
Keeps the device at the normal Windows policy baseline.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want standard Windows behavior and do not need to lock the feature down with policy.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are trying to hard-disable clipboard retention or device-to-device sync.
tradeoffs: Feature availability remains under Windows and user settings.
Does not enforce a privacy-focused clipboard policy.

recommended_profiles.label: Privacy focused
avoid_for: Users who rely on Win+V history

states.rationale: Disables cross-device clipboard sync by policy.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want copied content to stay out of clipboard history and cloud-backed sync paths.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You use Clipboard History as part of your daily workflow.
tradeoffs: Win+V history is no longer available.

recommended_profiles.label: Local history only
intended_for: People who do not want device-to-device sync
avoid_for: Strictly managed environments that ban clipboard retention

states.rationale: Blocks cross-device clipboard sync while keeping local history allowed.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Win+V history but do not want synced clipboard content moving between devices.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the simplest possible default policy or a full privacy lock-down.
tradeoffs: Local clipboard history is still retained on the device.
The app does not currently expose this mixed profile as a first-class preset.

evidence.title: Microsoft Policy CSP: AllowClipboardHistory
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the AllowClipboardHistory policy value and its supported values.

evidence.title: Microsoft Policy CSP: AllowCrossDeviceClipboard
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the AllowCrossDeviceClipboard policy value and its supported values.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft OSPolicy.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file shows both AllowCrossDeviceClipboard and AllowClipboardHistory under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes both policy values to 0 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: Current repo provenance already links this tweak to nohuto references and internal audit output.

decision.why: The local Microsoft OSPolicy.admx file explicitly defines AllowCrossDeviceClipboard and AllowClipboardHistory under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0, which matches the app's machine-policy bundle.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="AllowCrossDeviceClipboard" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System" valueName="AllowCrossDeviceClipboard"> ... <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue> <disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>; <policy name="AllowClipboardHistory" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System" valueName="AllowClipboardHistory"> ... <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue> <disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>
validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file names the same policy key and both value names used by the app, and it confirms the shared 1/0 mapping.
